coredata.dc:title: Influence of carbon nanodots on DNA-Thionine interaction. Application to breast cancer diagnosis
coredata.dc:creator: García-Mendiola, Tania
Requena-Sanz, Sergio
Martínez-Periñán, Emiliano
Bravo, Iria
Pariente, Félix
Lorenzo, Encarnación
coredata.dc:description: In this work we present for the first time the effect of carbon nanodots on the interaction of DNA layers deposited on gold electrodes and Thionine. Spectroscopic, electrochemical and operando spectroelectrochemistry data demonstrate that Thionine interacts with ssDNA or dsDNA to a different extent, depending on the presence or not of carbon nanodots on the electrode surface. Carbon nanodots serve as nanosubstrate for direct immobilization of non-functionalized oligonucleotide, due to the strong interaction of DNA bases with the nanomaterial. The acquired knowledge is applied to develop a new carbon nanodots based disposable DNA biosensor for the selective mutation detection in BRCA1 gene directly in clinical samples, using Thionine as redox indicator.
coredata.dcterms:subject: DNA interaction
Carbon nanodots influence
Spectroelectrochemistry
Mutation detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: DNA extraction and purification from peripheral blood samples and PCR amplification
Influence of CDs in the interaction of DNA and Th

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge Spanish Ministerio de Econom?a, Industria y Competitividad (CTQ2017-84309-C2-1-R), Ministerio de Ciencia, Innovaci?n y Universidades (RED2018-102412-T) and Comunidad de Madrid (S2018/NMT-4349) for financial support.

The authors acknowledge Spanish Ministerio de Economía, Industria y Competitividad ( CTQ2017-84309-C2-1-R ), Ministerio de Ciencia, Innovación y Universidades ( RED2018-102412-T ) and Comunidad de Madrid ( S2018/NMT-4349 ) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Cyclic voltammograms of 1.0 mM Th in 0.1 M PB pH 7.0 at AuSPE (A) or CDs/AuSPE (C). Cyclic voltammograms in 0.1 M PB pH 7.0 after Th accumulation at AuSPE (B) or CDs/AuSPE (D). Bare electrode (black line), dsDNA modified electrode (blue line) and ssDNA modified electrode (red line). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Successive UV–Vis (ΔAbs) spectra obtained during 25 cyclic potential scans (0.0 to −0.8 V (vs Ag/AgCl) at 25 mVs−1 of AuSPE (A), ssDNA/AuSPE (B), dsDNA/AuSPE (C), CDs/AuSPE (D), ssDNA/CDs/AuSPE (E) and dsDNA/CDs/AuSPE (F) immersed in a 150 μM Th 0.1 M PB pH 7.0.

Successive UV–Vis (ΔAbs) spectra obtained during the first cyclic potential scan (0.0 to −0.8 V (vs Ag/AgCl) at 25 mVs−1 of AuSPE (A), ssDNA/AuSPE (B), dsDNA/AuSPE (C), CDs/AuSPE (D), ssDNA/CDs/AuSPE (E) and dsDNA/CDs/AuSPE (F) immersed in a 150 μM Th 0.1 M PB pH 7.0. Inset: corresponding cyclic potential scans (0.0 to −0.8 V (vs Ag/AgCl)) at 25 mVs−1 of modified SPE immersed in a 150 μM Th 0.1 M PB pH 7.0.

Scheme of the biosensor development and peak current bar diagrams of the nanostructured biosensor response before (PROBEBRCA1) and after hybridization with fully complementary (WTBRCA1) or mutated (MUTBRCA1) sequences using Th as redox indicator.
Schematic representation of Th/DNA interaction on AuSPE (A) and CDs/AuSPE electrodes (B).
DNA sequences used in this work.

head.ce:title: Influence of carbon nanodots on DNA-Thionine interaction. Application to breast cancer diagnosis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departamento de Química Analítica y Análisis Instrumental, Universidad Autónoma de Madrid, 28049, Madrid, Spain

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Departamento de Química Analítica y Análisis Instrumental

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Departamento de Quimica Analitica y Analisis Instrumental, Universidad Autónoma de Madrid, 28049 Madrid, Spain

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Advanced Research in Chemical Sciences (IAdChem), Universidad Autónoma de Madrid, 28049, Madrid, Spain

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Advanced Research in Chemical Sciences (IAdChem)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Advanced Research in Chemical Sciences (IAdChem), Universidad Autónoma de Madrid, 28049 Madrid, Spain

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Instituto Madrileño de Estudios Avanzados en Nanociencia (IMDEA-Nanociencia), Faraday, 9, Campus UAM, Cantoblanco, 28049, Madrid, Spain

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Instituto Madrileño de Estudios Avanzados en Nanociencia (IMDEA-Nanociencia)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Instituto Madrileno de Estudios Avanzados en Nanociencia (IMDEA-Nanociencia), Faraday, 9, Campus UAM, Cantoblanco, 28049 Madrid, Spain

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work we present for the first time the effect of carbon nanodots on the interaction of DNA layers deposited on gold electrodes and Thionine. Spectroscopic, electrochemical and operando spectroelectrochemistry data demonstrate that Thionine interacts with ssDNA or dsDNA to a different extent, depending on the presence or not of carbon nanodots on the electrode surface. Carbon nanodots serve as nanosubstrate for direct immobilization of non-functionalized oligonucleotide, due to the strong interaction of DNA bases with the nanomaterial. The acquired knowledge is applied to develop a new carbon nanodots based disposable DNA biosensor for the selective mutation detection in BRCA1 gene directly in clinical samples, using Thionine as redox indicator.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots (CDs) are a recent member of the nanocarbon family that consist on spherically shaped nanoparticles essentially composed of naturally abundant and affordable elements, such as carbon, oxygen and hydrogen [1]. They show interesting properties, being the most remarkable solubility, biocompatibility, good conductivity and high luminescence [2]. Due to all these outstanding features, nowadays carbon nanodots-modified detection systems represent a chief driver towards the adaption of electrochemical methods for sensing applications [3–6]. Besides the increase in the surface area, the new functional groups incorporated to the nanostructured surface through the nanomaterial can be useful for the recognition element immobilization [7]. On the other hand, electrode nanostructuration with sp2 carbon nanomaterials, can lead to faster electron transfer kinetics [8].

It is well known that most diagnostic methods in human genetic diseases are based on the detection of mutations in the altered gene(s). For example, breast and ovarian cancer [9] are related to alterations in the breast cancer gene (BRCA1), which encodes a protein that acts as a tumor suppressor. One of the most common mutations associated with breast cancer is a specific mutation of the exon 9 of this gene, consisting on a change of a cytosine by a thymine. The presence of this alteration is associated with the probability of suffering this disease. In this sense, the detection of this alteration can be a rapid tool for cancer diagnosis. An early cancer diagnosis increases the survival rate. Usually, these alterations are identified by sequencing the whole gene and the way to detect mutations is DNA sequencing, by the classic Sanger method, but it is frequently a laborious task and costly method as routine diagnosis tool. Electrochemical DNA biosensors, as portable and simple devices, would be a good alternative [10–12]. The efficiency of these devices is largely conditioned by the possibility of having a selective and sensitive redox indicator of hybridization event [13]. Hence, the study of the interaction between DNA and small molecules is an interesting research area.

Thionine (3,7-diaminophenothiazin-5-ium) (Th) is a cationic dye with a planar structure that belongs to the phenothiazinium family that interacts with dsDNA mainly by intercalation [14–19], with a component of stabilization by H bonding [17,18]. Although the interaction studies of Th with the DNA molecule under various conditions have been well reported, the effect of the presence of nanomaterials in that interaction have not been reported yet, neither its application as hybridization indicator to the development of DNA electrochemical biosensors.

In the present work, we have performed for the first time an exhaustive study of the interaction of Th with a DNA layer and the influence of the presence of CDs by combining electrochemical with spectroscopy studies, including operando UV–Vis spectroelectrochemistry. We have used this study as a starting point to apply the acquired knowledge to develop a new electrochemical DNA biosensor for the selective mutation’s detection in BRCA1 gene, using Th as hybridization indicator and CDs as nanosubstrate for direct DNA probe immobilization. The advantages the developed biosensor are (1) previously functionalized DNA probes are not required and (2) an improvement in the electron transfer [6], leading to a higher sensitivity, selectivity and versatility of the resulting detection system The applicability of the biosensor has been demonstrated by analyzing clinical samples.

Sodium phosphate, sodium chloride, Thionine, double stranded calf thymus DNA (ds-DNA) and all other chemicals used in this work were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Co. (www.sigmaldrich.com). Water was purified with a Millipore Milli-Q-System (18.2 MΩ cm). Ds-DNA stock solutions (1.0 mg mL−1) were prepared in 0.1 M phosphate buffer (PB) pH 7.0 solution. The concentration in base pairs (bp) of DNA was determined by using the molar absorptivity (6600 L mol−1 cm−1) of DNA at 260 nm. Single stranded calf thymus DNA (ss-DNA) was obtained by boiling in water vials containing ds-DNA for 30 min and subsequently cooling them in an ice-bath. The resulting denatured DNA samples were stored frozen at −20 °C. Single stranded synthetic oligonucleotide, 110-mer, from sequences of BRCA1 gene (PROBEBRCA1) was supplied by Sigma-Aldrich Co. (see Table 1 ). Clinical samples of gene BRCA1 (wild type (WTBRCA1) and mutated (MUTBRCA1) were supplied and checked by Dr. Jesús Solera from La Paz Hospital (Madrid, Spain) and are also listed in Table 1.

A PharmaSpec UV-1700 series Shimadzu Corporation UV–Visible spectrophotometer and a Cary Eclipse Varian spectrofluorimeter were used for spectrophotometric and fluorimetric measurements, respectively. A BIO-RAD thermocycler (DNA Engine Tetrad 2. Peltier Thermal Cycler. BIO-RAD Laboratories) was used to generate PCR fragments. Electrochemical measurements were carried out with an Autolab PGSTAT 30 potentiostat from Eco-Chemie using the software package GPES 4.9. Screen-printed gold electrodes (AuSPEs) were supplied by DropSens (Spain). AuSPEs integrates a gold working, silver pseudoreference and gold counter electrodes. Hence, in the cyclic voltammetry and spectroelectrochemical experiments an external CHI-111 Ag/AgCl reference electrode (from CH Instruments) was employed in combination with the screen-printed electrodes to control the potential. A screen-printed electrode Connector (DropSens) was used as interface.

Spectroelectrochemical measurements were carried out using a Spelec system (Metrohm DropSens) using the DROPVIEWSPELEC Software. This system is equipped with a Bipotentiostat/Galvanostat (±4 V DC potential range, ±40 mA maximum measurable current) and a Spectrometer wavelength range: 200–900 nm. The experiments were carried out in reflection mode, using a Teflon reflection cell (Metrohm DropSens).

CDs have been synthetized by a hydrothermal method using citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors as described Yang et al. [20]. This nanomaterial has been synthetized and characterized by different techniques as we previously described [21].
The interaction of DNA and Th was studied by spectroscopic, electrochemical and operando UV–Vis spectroelectrochemistry techniques.

Spectroscopic studies . UV–visible absorption spectra were performed using 0.1 M PB pH 7.0 in 1.0 cm quartz cells. Absorbance titrations were carried out at 10 μM of Thionine and varying the concentration of DNA from 0 to 50 μM. The absorbance was plotted vs DNA concentration in base pairs. The intrinsic binding constants, Kb, were determined using the following equation:
[ADN] / (εa- εf) = [ADN] / (εb- εf) + 1 / Kb (εa- εf)
where εa is the molar absorptivity of Thionine in presence of the different concentrations of DNA and εf and εb are the molar absorptivity for free and bound forms of Thionine, respectively.

Electrochemical studies . AuSPE surfaces were cleaned and activated by cyclic voltammetry in 0.5 M H2SO4, 10 cycles. Immediately, the surfaces were modified with CDs and DNA or only DNA. To modify AuSPE with CDs 10 μL of a solution of the synthetized CDs were drop casted onto the electrode surface (CDs/AuSPE) and left overnight. After that, the resulting electrodes were modified by drop casting 10 μL a solution of ds or ssDNA 1.0 mg mL-1 (ds-ssDNA/CDs/AuSPE). The non-modified with CDs AuSPE electrodes were modified with DNA following the same procedure, drop casting 10 μL of a solution of ds or ssDNA 1.0 mg mL−1 (ds-ssDNA/AuSPE) and left overnight.

Operando UV–Vis spectroelectrochemistry. Electrodes were modified in the same way described for the electrochemical studies. Thionine solution was diluted from 1.0 mM to 150 μM in pH 7 0.1 M PB so that the electrochemical and UV–Vis spectroscopy responses could be simultaneously measured. The different modified electrodes were analyzed by cycling the potential between 0.00 V and −0.80 V at 0.025 V s−1 (vs Ag/AgCl) and recording the cyclic voltammetry response and the successive UV–Vis reflection spectra along the potential cycles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DNA extraction and purification from peripheral blood samples and PCR amplification
ce:section.ce:para: Clinical samples of genomic DNA were isolated from peripheral blood leukocytes from breast cancer patients by standardized procedures (commercial Kit Purogene from Qiagen GmbH, Hilden, Germany). After that, the first step in the approach involved the analysis by PCR amplification of each canonical exon, using oligonucleotides annealing to the flanking introns. PCR fragments were generated in a reaction volume of 75 μL, containing 200 ng of genomic DNA, 20 mM Tris (hydroxymethyl)aminomethane (Tris)–HCl (pH 8.4), 50 mM KCl, 2.5 mM MgCl2, 0.2 mM concentration of each deoxy-NTP, 0.4 μM concentration of each primer and 5 U of Taq Gold DNA polymerase (Life Technologies). The PCR consisted of an initial denaturation step at 95 °C for 3 min, followed by 16 cycles of denaturation at 94 °C for 30 s, annealing at 63 °C for 30 s decreasing 0.5 °C each cycle, and extension at 72 °C for 60 s, followed by 16 cycles of denaturation at 94 °C for 30 s, annealing at 55 °C for 30 s each cycle, and extension at 72 °C for 60 s with a final elongation step at 72 °C for 10 min. The PCR product was electrophoresed in a 1.5% agarose gel in Tris–borate–EDTA, and bands detected with ethidium bromide, and all PCR products were sequenced by Sanger. The analysis of all coding exons was performed, in order to explore every potential mutated allele on BRCA1 gene. In addition, Multiplex ligation-dependent probe amplification or MLPA analysis was carried out to identify any potential genomic deletion or duplication, using MLPA kits (MRC-Holland) and following manufacturers’ instructions. Finally, PCR products were purified with a QIAquick extraction kit (Qiagen GmbH, Hilden, Germany) according to the manufacturer’s instructions to remove traces of buffer, primers and deoxy-NTP. The clinical samples consisted on wild type (WTBRCA1) and mutated (MUTBRCA1) forms of BRCA1 gene.

Firstly, the electrodes were modified by drop casting 10 μL of a CDs stock solution (CDs/AuSPE). Afterwards, 10 μL of 10 μM PROBEBRCA1 synthetic sequence solution were deposited on the CDs/AuSPE. Afterwards, the electrode was kept at room temperature for 24 h. Then, it was soaked in water for 30 min. The resulting modified electrodes are named as PROBEBRCA1/CDs/AuSPE.
DNA samples obtained by PCR amplification were denatured by heating up to 100 °C followed by rapid cooling.

The PROBEBRCA1 immobilized on the gold electrode (PROBEBRCA1/CDs/AuSPE) was hybridized for 1 h at 40 °C with the clinical samples of gen BRCA1 (10 μL of a 5.0 ng μL−1 denatured clinical sample), consisting of a wild type (WTBRCA1) or a mutated form (MUTBRCA1) of BRCA1 gene. The resulting modified electrodes are named as WTBRCA1/PROBE/CDs/AuSPE and MUTBRCA1/PROBE/CDs/AuSPE, respectively. After hybridization, Th was accumulated on the DNA layer on the electrode surface by immersing the modified electrodes in 0.1 M PB pH 7.0 containing 1.0 mM of Thionine and cycling the potential (100 times) at 100 mV s−1. Then, the electrodes were rinsed with sterile water, placed in 0.1 M PB pH 7.0 and differential pulse voltammograms were immediately recorded.

CDs used in this work have been synthetized by a hydrothermal method using citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors [20] and characterized by different techniques (TEM, AFM, FTIR and UV–visible spectroscopy, fluorescence) as we have previously described [21]. From these studies we concluded that CDs have an average size of 4.1 ± 0.3 nm (Fig. 1SM). FTIR spectrum confirmed the presence of different functional groups (hydroxide, NH, CH) surrounding CDs, which is very interesting for further interaction with specific molecules or biomolecules endowing them with specific properties. CDs were also stable over at least 4 months with minor changes in the fluorescence spectral profiles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of CDs in the interaction of DNA and Th

ce:section.ce:para: Understanding the mechanisms of Th binding to DNA is of great interest in biological and medical research. This interest is justified taking into account that a large number of dyes that selectively interact with DNA possess pharmacological actions and therapeutic effects of considerable importance. In addition, they can be employed as redox indicators of the hybridization event. Th-DNA interaction studies generally analyze the spectral and viscosity changes induced by the dye. However, no electrochemical studies have been reported on the interaction of Th with a DNA layer deposited on a gold electrode, in spite of its potential application to the development of electrochemical DNA biosensors. On other hand, CDs can react with DNA backbone through H-bonding and π–π interactions [21] and this fact could affect the interaction of DNA with Th, if they are present. Hence, in the present work we tried to understand if carbon nanodots (CDs) can modify the interaction of DNA and Th. For this purpose, we have employed CDs as electrode modifiers. In a first step, we modified the electrode surface with CDs as described in Experimental section. Different amounts of CDs were used in order to confirm electrode surface coverage. Best results were obtained when 10 μL of CDs stock solution was used. Then, we studied the electrochemical behavior of Th at the resulting CDs nanostructured electrodes before and after electrode modification with DNA. Some studies have demonstrated that ssDNA adsorbs faster and stronger onto sp2 carbon nanomaterials than dsDNA [22,23], since in the dsDNA double helix structure only the phosphate groups are exposed, thus hampering π–π interactions. Hence, we performed the studies both with ssDNA and dsDNA. The results were compared to those obtained when electrodes without CDs are employed (see Scheme 1 ). To carry out these studies, gold screen printed electrodes (AuSPEs) were employed as drop-on sensors with the idea to further develop portable and disposable DNA sensing devices.

Fig. 1 A shows the electrochemical behavior of 1.0 mM Th in 0.1 M PB pH 7.0 at a bare AuSPE (black line), a dsDNA modified AuSPE (dsDNA/AuSPE) (blue line) and a ssDNA modified AuSPE (ssDNA/AuSPE) (red line). It can be seen that, at AuSPE (black line) a pair of redox peaks, ascribed to the oxidation/reduction of the dye appears at a formal potential (E°’) of – 0.384 V (vs Ag/AgCl). The peak potential separation (ΔEp), at 100 mV s−1, is 45 mV, which is slightly higher than that expected for a freely diffusing two-electron reversible redox process (Fig. 2SM). At the dsDNA/AuSPE (see Fig. 1A blue line), the E°’ shifted to −0.543 V (vs Ag/AgCl) and at the ssDNA/AuSPE (Fig. 1A red line) E°’ shifted to more negative values (−0.573 V vs Ag/AgCl). These results are consistent with a strong interaction between the dye and the DNA immobilized on the electrode surface [24,25]. It is worth to note that when the electrode is modified with DNA, in particular with dsDNA, a less reversible shape, as well as a decrease in the peak current, is also observed. This result suggests limitations in the charge transfer kinetics. Therefore, it seems that Th suffers steric or electrostatic impediments to electronic transfer when the electrode is modified with DNA. In order to determine the nature of the electrochemical process, the variation of peak current with the scan rate was studied. In the case of AuSPE, the peak current was directly proportional to the square root of the scan rate (v1/2), as expected for a diffusion process. On the other hand, in the case of DNA modified electrodes the peak current was directly proportional to the scan rate (v), which is characteristic of a surface confined redox process. This result confirms that Th is accumulated on the DNA layer immobilized on the electrode surface. Actually, there is an increase in the peak current on the successive potential cycling (see Fig. 3SM A and B), indicating that after each potential cycle the dye is accumulated on the DNA layer present at the electrode surface, especially when the electrode is modified with ssDNA (Fig. 3SM B). This result hint again at a stronger interaction of the dye with the ssDNA than with dsDNA. If after Th accumulation by potential cycles, the electrode is removed from the cell, rinsed with water and placed in 0.1 M PB pH 7.0 without Th, the characteristic redox couple ascribed to Th is observed (see Fig. 1B). This ratifies that the dye has been incorporated to the DNA layer present on the electrode surface. Additionally, as can be observed, the redox signal is higher in the case of ssDNA, confirming again a stronger interaction of Th with ssDNA than with dsDNA, resulting in a higher accumulation of the dye in the DNA backbone.

From the experiments discussed above, it can be concluded that the results obtained by electrochemical experiments point out to a strong interaction between Th and DNA, and that this interaction is stronger with ssDNA than with dsDNA.

When the electrode is modified with CDs (CDs/AuSPE; black line of Fig. 1C) the redox peak ascribed to the oxidation/reduction of Th appears at a formal potential (E°’) of −0.570 V (vs Ag/AgCl). This value is more negative than the one obtained in the case of an electrode without CDs (see black line of Fig. 1A) probably due to the less conductive properties of CDs compared with the bare gold electrode. It is also observed that when the nanostructured electrode is modified with DNA, there is a shift in the formal potential to more negative values. E°’ shifted to −0.580 V and to −0.695 V (vs Ag/AgCl) in the case of ssDNA and dsDNA, respectively. It is worth to note that, although as described above this also happen for electrodes without CDs, when the electrode is modified with CDs the shift is more accused for dsDNA, just the opposite to the case of an electrode without CDs. Hence, these results suggest that when CDs are present the DNA-Th interaction changes. In this case, Th interacts preferentially with dsDNA, whereas in the absence of CDs, with ssDNA.

As one would expect, after repetitive potential cycles (See Fig. 3SM) there is an increase in the peak current, confirming the accumulation of Th in the immobilized DNA layer. If after repetitive cycling, the electrode is removed from the cell, rinsed with water, and placed in electrolyte solution containing no Th, a pair of voltammetric peaks ascribed to the dye are observed (see Fig. 1D). This indicates that the incorporated Th remained bound to the DNA layer on the electrode surface. As can be observed, the peak intensity of the accumulated Th is much higher for the DNA/CDs/AuSPE than for the DNA/AuSPE (compare Fig. 1B and D), suggesting a greater Th accumulation on the DNA backbone when the electrode is modified with CDs. In addition, it is also observed that Th accumulates more on the dsDNA than on the ssDNA layer, confirming again that CDs could disturb the way DNA (ss or ds) interacts with Th. Although largely speculative by our part, we suggest that Th can interact with DNA by free guanines or by intercalation between the base pair, but with some preference to free bases of ssDNA as we can observe in the case of AuSPEs. In the case of CDs/AuSPEs, this affinity changes due to the interaction between DNA and CDs [21], which in some extent blocks the Th interaction with the DNA (see Scheme 1). This could be due to the stronger adsorption of ssDNA onto sp2 carbon nanomaterials than dsDNA [22,23], as we have mentioned above.

From all these results we can summarize that when the electrode is nanostructured with CDs the peak intensity corresponding to Th accumulated to the DNA layer is much higher than in absence of CDs, suggesting a greater amount of Th accumulated on the DNA backbone. This effect can be in part explained by the increase in the relative electrode surface area caused by the nanomaterial. Furthermore, Th accumulation on the ssDNA layer is lower than on the dsDNA layer, opposite to what happens in the absence of CDs. In this case the interaction of CDs with DNA backbone through H-bonding and π–π interactions [21] seems to play a major role in hindering interaction with Th.

In order to elucidate the role of CDs in the interaction between DNA (ss and ds) and Th, an in-depth spectroelectrochemical analysis was carried out. The combination operando spectrophotometric and electrochemical techniques to analyze the surface process can provide a valuable information that would not be obtained by performing both techniques separately. At a first step, before performing this analysis, we have studied the spectrophotometric behavior of Th in the absence and in presence of DNA. From the electronic spectrum of the Th in the absence and presence of increasing concentrations of ss or dsDNA (Fig. 4SM) a hypochromic effect and a red shift, more accused in the case of ssDNA, after DNA addition is observed. These DNA-induced spectral variations can be explained in terms of changes in the local polarity around the chromophore due to strong interactions with pyrimidine and purine bases [26]. Hence, the results obtained direct attention to a strong interaction between Th and DNA, as it has been concluded from the cyclic voltammetry and suggest that it is stronger with the individual bases of ssDNA than dsDNA, which is similar to that described by us for other dyes [27]. The absorption spectra changes observed were used to estimate the respective binding constants. According to Equation 1 (see experimental section), the respective intrinsic binding constants (Kb) were estimated to be 1.00 × 104 M-1 and 1.40 × 106 M-1 for dsDNA and ssDNA respectively, confirming again that Th has a strong affinity for the ssDNA and interacts in a different way with ss and ds-DNA [28,29].

The operando UV–Vis spectroelectrochemistry allows us to obtain simultaneously absorption and electrochemical data, to better characterize the electrode surface while the electrochemical process is taking place. Fig. 2 shows the absorbance variation spectra along the electrochemical potential scans. In the case of AuSPE (Fig. 2A), a decrease of the bands at 564, 591 and 675 nm took place during the successive potential scans. This decrease can be considered a consequence of the interaction of Th and reduced Th with the AuSPE during the successive potential scans. In the presence of CDs (Fig. 2D), the same decreasing bands appear but the absorbance variation is lower, probably due to the lower conductivity of the CDs modified electrode compared with the bare one and the subsequent less interaction of Th with the electrode platform. In the case of DNA modified AuSPE, we observe a different behavior depending on DNA hybridization status. Absorbance variations are observed at 564, 591 (decrease) and 625 nm (increase) either for ss (Fig. 2B) or dsDNA (Fig. 2C). Since the increasing band at 625 nm is not detected when DNA is not present, we can conclude that it is associated to the DNA-Th interaction. This fact agrees well with the wavelength red shift observed during the interaction of DNA and Th in solution (Fig. 4SM A and B), both for ssDNA and dsDNA.

The absorbance decrease of 564 and 591 bands observed in operando studies in the case of ssDNA/AuSPEs is not as high as in the case of the bare AuSPE, whereas the absorbance at 625 nm presents an important increase along the potential scans, indicating the preferential interaction of Th with ssDNA. On the other hand, dsDNA/AuSPE shows a high decrease at 564 and 591 nm (of the same magnitude as for the bare AuSPE), whereas the absorbance increase at 625 nm is not as high as for the ssDNA/AuSPE, what indicates the lower availability of dsDNA to interact with Th and the subsequent interaction of the phenothiazine with the gold electrode. When AuSPEs have been previously modified with CDs, a surprising result is obtained. SsDNA/CDs/AuSPE shows the absorbance of 564 and 591 nm bands decreases in a higher magnitude, whereas the absorbance increase at 625 nm is smaller than that observed for ssDNA/AuSPE. This fact can be explained by the interaction of DNA with CDs, which partially hampers its interaction with Th during the electroaccumulation process. Hence, the higher quantity of free Th available to interact with gold causes the higher decrease of 564 and 591 nm bands.

In the case of dsDNA/CDs/AuSPE, the absorbance at 564 and 591 nm is lower, whereas the increase at 625 nm is higher than the values obtained for dsDNA/AuSPE. Like in the case of ssDNA/CDs/AuSPE, these results may be explained considering the interaction of dsDNA with CDs, which strongly affect its interaction with Th.

Results obtained from spectroelectrochemistry agree well with those obtained above by CV and point to the fact that in presence of CDs, Th interacts with dsDNA stronger than with ssDNA, whereas in the absence of CDs the strong interaction is with ssDNA.

Further information about the interaction process of Th with DNA can be obtained by observing the spectroelectrochemical data obtained during the first potential scan of the electroaccumulative process. As can be observed, when dsDNA is present (dsDNA/AuSPE and dsDNA/CDs/AuSPE; Fig. 3 C and F, respectively) the band at 625 nm increases more compared with ssDNA, which indicates a faster interaction of Th with dsDNA than with ssDNA. Moreover, in the case of ssDNA the increase of 625 nm band needs some potential scans to be detectable, whereas the decrease of 564 and 591 nm bands (associated with the interaction of gold and Th) is visible from the first scan of the electroaccumulation process. Thus, we can conclude that the interaction of Th is faster with dsDNA than with ssDNA in all cases, although along the potential scans the preference is to interact with ssDNA in the absence of CDs and with the dsDNA in the presence of CDs.

Spectroelectrochemical experiments confirm that in the absence of CDs, thionine interacts preferentially with ssDNA instead of dsDNA, and provide valuable and additional insight about this interaction. This information can be a point of departure for later applications in sensing and diagnosis.

On the basis of above results, we can also conclude that CDs nanostructuration provides a higher accumulation of Th on the DNA layer. This fact could be especially useful to improve the performance of DNA electrochemical biosensors. Furthermore, the functional groups incorporated to the nanostructured surface through the nanomaterial can be useful for the recognition element immobilization for DNA biosensor development avoiding the use of chemically modified DNA sequences.

Finally, we wanted to extend further all the knowledge acquired in the above-described studies to the development of a new disposable DNA biosensing platform for breast cancer diagnosis, It is based on the detection of a cancer biomarker, in particular, a mutation in gene BRCA1, using Th as redox indicator (see the Scheme in Fig. 4 ) and CDs as modifiers for the recognition element immobilization. The procedure for the prototype device fabrication is simple. The first step was the immobilization of the capture probe, a synthetic single stranded 100-mer sequence of this gene (PROBEBRCA1) directly onto the carbon nanodots surface of the disposable electrode (CDs/AuSPE) by drop casting, based on the strong interaction of nucleobases and carbon nanodots, as we previously described [6]. The presence of CDs avoids the use of previously functionalized probes. The next step, after capture probe immobilization onto the CDs/AuSPE, was the recognition of the target ssDNA and mutation detection based on the hybridization event using Th as redox indicator and Differential Pulse Voltammetry (DPV) to increase the sensitivity of the method. The DNA biosensor developed was evaluated by analyzing genomic DNA (220-mer PCR amplicons) extracted from human blood cells of healthy people (amplicons of the wild type form) or disease patients carrying a mutation in gene BRCA1 (the mutated form), Table 1. The biosensor response was obtained from the corresponding DPV signal of Th accumulated on the dsDNA layer formed on the CDs present at the electrode surface after hybridization with the mutated (MUTBRCA1) or wild type (WTBRCA1) PCR amplicons. Fig. 4 shows the bar diagram of the biosensor response for the WTBRCA1 and MUTBRCA1 samples. As can be seen, when the hybridization takes place with the mutated sequence, the signal obtained is less than a half of that obtained with the wild type (see also Fig. 5SM). Considering that the reproducibility and the error associated to each measure (evaluated from the response of five different biosensors prepared in the same manner) is of 95% and less than 5%, respectively, it can be concluded that the proposed screening method efficiently discriminates between WTBRCA1 and MUTBRCA1 samples. Moreover, the electrochemical signal shows excellent correlation with the amount of the target sequence (WTBRCA1) up to 10.0 ng μL−1 (Fig. 6SM) and the detection limit was found to be 55.0 pg μL-1 (According to the 3 x Sb. m−1 criteria, where Sb corresponds to the standard deviation (n = 5) of measurements made with non-target DNA and m is the slope of the calibration plot). In order to confirm the main role of CDs we carried out the same experiment but skipping the nanostructuring process with CDs. In this case we employed bare AuSPEs (Fig. 7SM). No mutation can be detected, since the difference between wild type and mutated signals is not enough. Hence, CDs act as nanosubstrate for non-functionalized probe sequences immobilization in a disposition that allows the hybridization with the target DNA.

This platform can be employed as a disposable device for a rapid and simple screening of gene mutations as other biosensors reported in the literature, but due to the interaction of nucleobases with CDs, without the need of using previously chemically functionalized oligonucleotides as probe.

The interaction of DNA with Thionine at unmodified and modified carbon nanodots electrodes was studied by combining spectrophotometry, electrochemistry and operando spectroelectrochemistry. Results show that the presence of CDs makes the interaction of Thionine with dsDNA favorable, opposite to the tendency shown in absence of CDs.

The results served to fabricate an CDs nanostructured electrochemical platform as a starting point to develop a disposable DNA biosensor for the selective mutation’s detection in BRCA1 gene directly in clinical samples using thionine as redox indicator.

Tania García-Mendiola, Sergio Requena-Sanz, have carried out the experiments regarding DNA-Thionine-CDs interaction and biosensor development. They have also participated in the manuscript elaboration. Iria Bravo has synthetized and characterized Carbon nanodot. Emiliano Martínez-Periñán has carried out the experiments regarding spectroelectrochemistry studies. Felix Pariente and Encarnación Lorenzo have supervised the experimental results and have elaborate the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge Spanish Ministerio de Economía, Industria y Competitividad (CTQ2017-84309-C2-1-R), Ministerio de Ciencia, Innovación y Universidades (RED2018-102412-T) and Comunidad de Madrid (S2018/NMT-4349) for financial support.
